description: You are a database administrator who routinely works with MySQL that is deployed inside a Linux container. A single production-grade container named mysql-prod is already running on the host. Objective 1. Display the exact container ID (12-character hexadecimal string shown by Docker) of the container whose name is mysql-prod. 2. Persist the same ID to a log file at /home/user/output/mysql_container_id.log (create the directory if it does not yet exist). Required output format • On standard output: the 12-character container ID followed by a single newline — nothing else. • In the log file: the very same 12-character container ID followed by a newline — nothing else. (The automated test will fail if there are extra spaces, additional lines, prefixes, or suffixes.) Constraints • Achieve the entire objective with one logical terminal command (it may contain pipes or redirections but must be issued as a single line). • Do not use sudo; your user already has the privileges needed for Docker commands. • Ensure the file /home/user/output/mysql_container_id.log ends up with permission 644 (-rw-r--r--). Hints you may find useful (do not treat them as prescriptive): • docker ps --filter can restrict the listing to a specific container name. • The --format flag lets you print just the container ID. • You can write to a file and simultaneously print to the terminal with utilities such as tee.
truth: Pre-existing system state for verification 1. A single container with the following immutable properties is running before the agent starts: • human-readable name: mysql-prod • container ID (first 12 chars used by Docker): 87e5c1b4a2d3 2. No other containers match the name mysql-prod. 3. Directory /home/user/output does NOT exist at start-up. 4. Tests will confirm: • STDOUT from the final command is exactly the string “87e5c1b4a2d3\n”. • File /home/user/output/mysql_container_id.log exists, is world-readable (permission 644), and contains exactly “87e5c1b4a2d3\n”.
name: task_000000_cae025c2
